senses.short_gloss: a living organism — tree, flower, or grass — that grows in soil and light
senses.explanation: A plant is not just something green to put on a shelf. It is a living thing that pulls water up through its roots, turns sunlight into energy, and quietly grows. A tree, a flower, a blade of grass — all are plants. When a place is full of plants, it feels alive in a way that's hard to explain but easy to feel.

examples.text: She waters her plants every morning before work.
The garden was full of colorful plants that bloomed in spring.
A new plant was growing between the cracks in the sidewalk.

senses.short_gloss: to put seeds or a young plant into the ground so it can grow
senses.explanation: When you plant something, you give it a home in the earth. You dig a small hole, place the seed or root inside, and cover it gently. From that moment, rain and sun do the rest. Farmers plant crops every season. You can plant a tree today that will stand long after you are gone — a slow, quiet gift to the future.

examples.text: They planted tomatoes in the backyard every spring.
He knelt down and carefully planted the small seedling near the fence.
The city planted hundreds of new trees along the main road.

senses.short_gloss: the way something looks or is planned; the set of choices that shape its form and function
senses.explanation: The design of an object is everything someone decided about how it should look and how it should work. A good design is not just beautiful — it fits the hand, makes the task easier, and feels right without you noticing why. When you pick up a phone or sit in a chair and think 'someone thought carefully about this,' you are noticing good design.

examples.text: The new bus station won an award for its clever design.
She sketched out several designs before choosing the best one.
The design of the packaging made the product look expensive.

senses.short_gloss: to plan and decide how something will look or work before it is made
senses.explanation: To design something is to imagine it before it exists. You decide what shape it will take, what it will be made of, how a person will use it. Architects design buildings. Engineers design bridges. Every object you rely on was once just a thought in someone's head — someone had to sit down and design it from nothing.

examples.text: She was hired to design the company's new website.
The team spent three months designing the new product.
He designed the entire kitchen himself, drawing every detail by hand.

senses.short_gloss: to prepare food using heat — frying, boiling, baking, or grilling it
senses.explanation: When you cook, you use heat to transform raw ingredients into something safe and delicious to eat. You might fry an egg in a pan, boil pasta in water, or slide a tray of vegetables into an oven. Cooking changes not just the temperature of food but its texture and flavor — something hard softens, something raw becomes rich and fragrant. It is one of the most practical and satisfying skills a person can have.

examples.text: She cooks dinner for her family every evening.
He learned to cook when he moved out on his own.
They cooked the fish over an open fire on the beach.

senses.short_gloss: a person whose job or skill is preparing food
senses.explanation: A cook is the person in charge of the kitchen. Whether in a family home or a busy restaurant, the cook is the one who decides what goes into a dish and how it is made. A skilled cook can take simple ingredients — eggs, flour, a pinch of salt — and turn them into something people will remember long after the meal is over.

examples.text: The restaurant hired a talented cook who had trained in France.
My grandmother was the best cook I have ever known.
The cook prepared everything from scratch using local ingredients.

senses.short_gloss: to take something temporarily from someone, with the plan to return it
senses.explanation: When you borrow something, you take it from someone else with the understanding that you will give it back. You borrow a book from a library and return it after reading. You borrow money from a friend and pay it back later. The key part is the promise — unlike taking, borrowing always carries the expectation of returning. It is built on trust.

examples.text: Can I borrow your umbrella? It looks like rain.
She borrowed a few books from the library to read over the weekend.
He borrowed money from his parents to pay for the course.

senses.short_gloss: feeling a need for rest because your body or mind has used up its energy
senses.explanation: Tired is that heavy feeling that settles in when you have done enough for the day. Your eyes want to close, your muscles feel slow and dull, and clear thinking becomes an effort. You can feel tired after a long run, a full day of work, or even a difficult conversation. Sleep is usually the only real fix — and when you are truly tired, it is the only thing that sounds good.

examples.text: After the long flight, she was too tired to do anything but sleep.
He looked tired, with dark circles under his eyes.
The children were so tired after the hike that they fell asleep in the car.

senses.short_gloss: to reach the place you were traveling toward
senses.explanation: To arrive is to complete a journey — to finally be at the place you were heading toward. After a long flight, you arrive at the airport. After an hour of walking, you arrive at the park. The word carries a sense of completion, of having moved through time and distance and landed somewhere new. The moment of arrival often feels like a small relief, a held breath finally released.

examples.text: The train is expected to arrive at eight in the evening.
They arrived at the hotel exhausted but excited.
She arrived early to make sure she had a good seat.

senses.short_gloss: the first meal of the day, eaten after waking up
senses.explanation: Breakfast is the meal you eat after waking, when your body has gone without food through the night. The word itself tells the story: you are breaking the fast of sleep. It might be as simple as a piece of fruit or as full as eggs, bread, and coffee. Many people say it is the most important meal of the day because it gives your body the fuel it needs to start moving and thinking clearly.

examples.text: She never leaves the house without eating breakfast first.
He made eggs and toast for breakfast while listening to the news.
The hotel offered a large breakfast buffet every morning.

senses.short_gloss: a person who lives next to you or very close to where you live
senses.explanation: Your neighbour is the person who lives closest to you — in the house next door, the flat above, or across the road. A neighbour is not a stranger, but not usually a close friend either. You share a wall, a staircase, or a stretch of street. Good neighbours look out for each other, wave hello, and sometimes lend a hand when things get difficult. Being a good neighbour is one of the small ways people make a community feel safe.

examples.text: Our neighbour kindly collected our mail while we were away.
She introduced herself to the new neighbours on the first day.
He could hear his neighbour playing music through the thin walls.

senses.short_gloss: the energy and work you deliberately put into doing something
senses.explanation: Effort is the fuel behind everything hard. It is the difference between doing a task carelessly and doing it well. When something is easy, it takes little effort. When something is difficult — learning a language, building a skill, finishing a demanding project — effort is what carries you forward. The result of almost any task reflects exactly how much effort went in.

examples.text: She put a lot of effort into preparing for the interview.
Learning a new language takes consistent effort over a long time.
Even a small effort every day can lead to big results over time.

senses.short_gloss: the shared beliefs, customs, language, and ways of life built by a group of people over time
senses.explanation: Culture is not one thing but many — the language people speak, the food they cook, the music they play, the stories they tell their children, the way they greet strangers. Every country, city, and even family has its own culture, and it shapes how people see the world in ways they often do not notice. To understand a culture is to start understanding the people who grew up inside it.

examples.text: Living abroad gave her a deep appreciation for other cultures.
The museum had an exhibition exploring the culture of ancient Egypt.
Food is one of the most visible parts of any culture.

senses.short_gloss: the reason something exists or the goal behind an action
senses.explanation: Purpose is the why behind a thing. A knife has the purpose of cutting. A school has the purpose of teaching. When people talk about their life's purpose, they mean something deeper — the reason they get up every morning, the direction they are moving in, the contribution they want to make. Having a clear sense of purpose makes hard work feel meaningful rather than just exhausting.

examples.text: The purpose of this meeting is to agree on the final budget.
She felt a strong sense of purpose when she was helping others.
Every room in the building was designed with a specific purpose in mind.

senses.short_gloss: a structure built over water or a gap so people or vehicles can cross from one side to the other
senses.explanation: A bridge solves a simple but important problem: there is a gap, and you need to get across. It might be a few wooden planks over a narrow stream or an enormous structure of steel and cable spanning hundreds of meters over a wide river. Without a bridge, you would have to go far around or swim. Beyond the physical meaning, people often use 'bridge' to describe anything that connects two separate things — a bridge between two cultures, two ideas, two people.

examples.text: The old stone bridge had stood over the river for more than three hundred years.
They built a new bridge to connect the island to the mainland.
Learning the local language helped her build a bridge with the community.
